title: Dieppe Raid (Operation Jubilee)

allied.strength: ~6,086 men — 2nd Canadian Division (~5,000), British Commandos, Royal Navy, RAF

casualties.allied: 3,623 Canadian casualties (killed, wounded, captured); 1,027 killed; 106 RAF aircraft lost

context: Dieppe was conceived as a test of German coastal defences and a demonstration of Allied ability to mount amphibious operations — partly to satisfy Soviet demands for a second front. The original raid (Operation Rutter) was cancelled in July 1942 after security was compromised; the decision to remount it as Operation Jubilee in August remains controversial. The plan's frontal assault on a heavily defended port violated principles that even 1942 planners understood: there was no preliminary heavy bombardment, there was no element of surprise after the first wave was detected by a German coastal convoy, the pebble beach immobilised the Churchill tanks, and the high cliffs gave German defenders a commanding field of fire. Of 4,963 Canadians who crossed the Channel, 916 were killed and 1,946 taken prisoner. The raid's lessons — on fire support, landing craft design, beach reconnaissance, port assault, and artificial harbours — directly shaped the planning for the Normandy landings two years later. Dieppe's catastrophe arguably saved tens of thousands of lives on D-Day.
outcome: German defensive victory. All objectives failed. Raid force withdrew after nine hours.
significance: Demonstrated the impossibility of a frontal assault on a defended port. Lessons directly shaped D-Day planning: preliminary bombardment requirements, artificial harbours (Mulberry), landing craft design, and beach reconnaissance.
summary: Operation Jubilee was a large-scale amphibious raid on the port of Dieppe. Of 6,086 men who embarked, only 2,210 returned to England. The 2nd Canadian Division suffered catastrophically on the beaches. German defenders — forewarned by chance naval encounter at sea, positioned in cliffs overlooking the beach, and reinforced by concrete fortifications — cut down the attackers as they struggled across pebble beaches under fire.

sources.ref: National Archives (UK). DEFE 2/334: Combined Operations HQ — Operation Jubilee Planning and Report, 1942.
Stacey, C.P. Six Years of War: The Army in Canada, Britain and the Pacific. Queen's Printer, 1955.
sources.note: The official Canadian military history of the Dieppe raid.

sources.ref: Neillands, Robin. The Dieppe Raid: The Story of the Disastrous 1942 Expedition. Indiana University Press, 2005.
